pathways.label: General conviction-record sealing under NRS 179.245
pathways.summary: The waiting period generally runs from release from actual custody, discharge from parole/probation, or release from suspended sentence, whichever applies and is later. The current NRS 179.245 waiting periods are: Residential burglary under NRS 205.060 10 years Category B, C, or D felony 5 years Non-felony Medicaid fraud / non-felony DUI / non-felony battery domestic violence 7 years Misdemeanor battery, harassment, stalking, or protection-order violation 2 years

ruleClauses: The waiting period generally runs from release from actual custody, discharge from parole/probation, or release from suspended sentence, whichever applies and is later. The current NRS 179.245 waiting periods are:
Residential burglary under NRS 205.060 10 years
Category B, C, or D felony 5 years
Non-felony Medicaid fraud / non-felony DUI / non-felony battery domestic violence 7 years
Misdemeanor battery, harassment, stalking, or protection-order violation 2 years

NRS 179.245 requires a current verified Nevada criminal-history record from the Central Repository and a list of agencies or custodians reasonably known to have the records. The petition must identify the conviction, date of birth, and arrest date tied to the conviction. (
"https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-245/")

During the applicable waiting period, the person must not have pending charges and must not have been convicted of another offense, except minor moving or standing traffic violations. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-245/")
Any new conviction since the case ended?
Any probation, parole, or suspended sentence still active?
NRS 179.245 conviction exclusions
A person may not seal a conviction under NRS 179.245 if it is:
Invasion of the home with a deadly weapon.
Vehicular homicide / DUI-related homicide.
Certain felony boating-under-the-influence offenses.
Certain boating DUI offenses. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-245/")

Nevada allows sealing for many convictions, but not all. Crimes against children, sexual offenses, certain felony DUI or DUI-death cases, and some other serious offenses are blocked or need attorney review.
Dismissed, declined, or acquitted cases: NRS 179.255 If charges were dismissed, declined, or resulted in acquittal, Nevada has a separate no-conviction pathway.

Acquittal Any time after acquittal

The petition must include the current verified criminal-history record, disposition, list of agencies/custodians, date of birth, specific charges, and arrest date. If the prosecutor stipulates, the court applies the sealing presumption. If there is no stipulation or objection within 30 days and statutory findings are met, the court may seal without a hearing; if there is an objection, a hearing is required. (
"https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-255/")

Prosecution declined After statute of limitations runs, or 8 years after arrest, or by stipulation

The petition must include the current verified criminal-history record, disposition, list of agencies/custodians, date of birth, specific charges, and arrest date. If the prosecutor stipulates, the court applies the sealing presumption. If there is no stipulation or objection within 30 days and statutory findings are met, the court may seal without a hearing; if there is an objection, a hearing is required. (

If your Nevada case was dismissed or you were acquitted, you may be able to petition right away. If prosecution was declined before charges were filed, the timing depends on the statute of limitations, eight years from arrest, or whether the prosecutor stipulates.

Under NRS 176A.245, 176A.265, and 176A.295, after certain probation discharges, dismissals, conditional dismissals, or set-asides, the court must order records sealed if the defendant fulfilled the terms and conditions, unless the Division petitions for good cause and requests a hearing. For certain domestic battery or DUI-related charges under these provisions, sealing may require waiting 7 years after conditional dismissal or set-aside and filing a petition. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-176a/statute-176a-245/")

Four years after an eligible person completes a reentry program, the court may seal records. The person must have successfully completed a qualifying reentry program and have been convicted of a single felony offense that did not involve use or threatened use of force or violence. Multiple felony convictions may count as a single offense if they arose out of the same transaction or occurrence. Crimes against children and sexual offenses are excluded. Professional licensing boards and the Division of Insurance may still inspect records sealed under this section for licensing/discipline purposes. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-259/")

Controlled-substance possession not for sale: NRS 453.3365 For certain convictions for possession of a controlled substance not for sale, the court may seal records three years after conviction and sentencing if the person completed court/parole/probation terms and the court is satisfied after hearing that the person is rehabilitated. If the person was discharged from probation under NRS 453.3363, the court must order sealing if terms were fulfilled, unless Parole and Probation petitions for good cause not to seal and requests a hearing. Professional licensing boards may inspect records sealed under this section. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-453/statute-453-3365/") Wilma status labels:possible_drug_possession_not_for_sale_453_3365possible_453_3363_discharge_sealinglicensing_board_access_warning

Decriminalized offense: NRS 179.271 If an offense is later decriminalized, a person convicted before decriminalization may submit a written request to the court that convicted them. If the prosecutor does not object within 10 judicial days, the court must grant the request. If the prosecutor objects, the court holds a hearing and must grant unless the prosecutor proves good cause by clear and convincing evidence. No fee may be charged. This pathway does not apply to traffic offenses. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-271/") Wilma status labels:possible_decriminalized_offense_no_feeexcluded_traffic_offense_decriminalization_routeprosecutor_10_judicial_day_objection_window

pathways.label: Deferred-judgment dismissal and sealing under NRS 176.211
pathways.summary: Nevada deferred judgment can allow a person to plead guilty, guilty but mentally ill, or no contest, but have judgment deferred. If the person completes the terms, the court discharges the person and dismisses the proceedings. The statute says the discharge/dismissal is without adjudication of guilt and is not a conviction for employment, civil-rights, licensing, questionnaire, and other public/private purposes, though it can still count for certain repeat-offense or bail purposes. The court must order records sealed if the person fulfills required terms, unless the Division or prosecutor petitions for good cause not to seal and requests a…
ruleClauses: Nevada deferred judgment can allow a person to plead guilty, guilty but mentally ill, or no contest, but have judgment deferred. If the person completes the terms, the court discharges the person and dismisses the proceedings. The statute says the discharge/dismissal is without adjudication of guilt and is not a conviction for employment, civil-rights, licensing, questionnaire, and other public/private purposes, though it can still count for certain repeat-offense or bail purposes. The court must order records sealed if the person fulfills required terms, unless the Division or prosecutor petitions for good cause not to seal and requests a hearing. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-176/statute-176-211/")

sourceSections.title: Core Nevada rule
sourceSections.text:
Nevada courts may seal criminal records if statutory conditions are met and the court is satisfied the person qualifies. Sealing restricts public dissemination, but it does not necessarily erase every copy everywhere, and out-of-state/federal agencies may not be fully bound by a Nevada order. ("https://www.rccd.nv.gov/fees-forms/criminal-history-sealing-of-records/information-on-the-sealing-of-nevada-criminal-history-records/")
Wilma plain-language script:
In Nevada, the usual remedy is record sealing, not true expungement. If granted, the record is removed from ordinary public access and the case is treated as if it did not happen for many purposes, but some agencies may still access sealed records under Nevada law.

sourceSections.title: Main conviction sealing: NRS 179.245
sourceSections.text:
Waiting periods
The waiting period generally runs from release from actual custody, discharge from parole/probation, or release from suspended sentence, whichever applies and is later. The current NRS 179.245 waiting periods are:
Conviction type Waiting period
Category A felony 10 years
Crime of violence 10 years
Residential burglary under NRS 205.060 10 years
Category B, C, or D felony 5 years
Category E felony 2 years
Gross misdemeanor 2 years
Non-felony Medicaid fraud / non-felony DUI / non-felony battery domestic violence 7 years
Misdemeanor battery, harassment, stalking, or protection-order violation 2 years
Any other misdemeanor 1 year
NRS 179.245 requires a current verified Nevada criminal-history record from the Central Repository and a list of agencies or custodians reasonably known to have the records. The petition must identify the conviction, date of birth, and arrest date tied to the conviction. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-245/")
Wilma status labels:likely_eligible_category_a_or_violence_10_yearslikely_eligible_b_c_d_felony_5_yearslikely_eligible_category_e_felony_2_yearslikely_eligible_gross_misdemeanor_2_yearslikely_eligible_other_misdemeanor_1_yearnot_eligible_yet_waiting_periodneeds_current_verified_chr

Clean-period rule
During the applicable waiting period, the person must not have pending charges and must not have been convicted of another offense, except minor moving or standing traffic violations. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-245/")
Wilma screen:Ask:
Any pending charges anywhere?
Any new conviction since the case ended?
Any open warrants?
Any probation, parole, or suspended sentence still active?

NRS 179.245 conviction exclusions
A person may not seal a conviction under NRS 179.245 if it is:
A crime against a child.
A sexual offense.
Invasion of the home with a deadly weapon.
Certain felony DUI offenses.
Vehicular homicide / DUI-related homicide.
Certain felony boating-under-the-influence offenses.
Certain boating DUI offenses. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-245/")
Wilma hard-stop / legal-review labels:excluded_crime_against_childexcluded_sexual_offenseexcluded_home_invasion_deadly_weaponexcluded_felony_duiexcluded_dui_homicide_or_vehicular_homicideexcluded_felony_bui_or_boating_duilegal_review_required_unclear_offense_category
Wilma script:
Nevada allows sealing for many convictions, but not all. Crimes against children, sexual offenses, certain felony DUI or DUI-death cases, and some other serious offenses are blocked or need attorney review.

4. Dismissed, declined, or acquitted cases: NRS 179.255
If charges were dismissed, declined, or resulted in acquittal, Nevada has a separate no-conviction pathway.

Outcome When petition may be filed
Charges dismissed Any time after dismissal
Acquittal Any time after acquittal
Prosecution declined After statute of limitations runs, or 8 years after arrest, or by stipulation
The petition must include the current verified criminal-history record, disposition, list of agencies/custodians, date of birth, specific charges, and arrest date. If the prosecutor stipulates, the court applies the sealing presumption. If there is no stipulation or objection within 30 days and statutory findings are met, the court may seal without a hearing; if there is an objection, a hearing is required. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-255/")
Wilma status labels:likely_eligible_dismissed_no_waitlikely_eligible_acquitted_no_waitdeclined_prosecution_wait_sol_or_8_years_or_stipulationneeds_disposition_documentneeds_statute_of_limitations_review
Wilma script:
If your Nevada case was dismissed or you were acquitted, you may be able to petition right away. If prosecution was declined before charges were filed, the timing depends on the statute of limitations, eight years from arrest, or whether the prosecutor stipulates.

sourceSections.title: Rebuttable presumption in favor of sealing
sourceSections.text:
Nevada has a helpful rule: for petitions under NRS 179.245, 179.247, 179.255, 179.259, or 179.2595, there is a rebuttable presumption that records should be sealed if the applicant satisfies all statutory requirements. The presumption does not apply to a defendant who received a dishonorable discharge from probation under NRS 176A.850. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-2445/")
Wilma script:
Nevada law creates a presumption in favor of sealing when the statutory requirements are met, but it is still not an automatic guarantee. The prosecutor or another party may object, and the court may need to hold a hearing.

sourceSections.title: Effect of a Nevada sealing order
sourceSections.text:
Once records are sealed under the listed Nevada sealing statutes, the proceedings are generally deemed never to have occurred, and the person may answer accordingly to inquiries, including employment applications. Sealing also immediately restores the civil rights to vote, hold office, and serve on a jury if those rights had not already been restored. However, sealing does not restore firearm rights unless the person receives a pardon that does not restrict the right to bear arms. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-285/")
Wilma must say:
Record sealing does not restore gun rights by itself.

Wilma should not say:
This fixes firearms, immigration, licensing, or federal background-check issues.

sourceSections.title: Who may still inspect sealed records?
sourceSections.text:
Some sealed records may still be inspected by authorized entities. Nevada law allows access in specific circumstances for the Gaming Control Board/Gaming Commission, Division of Insurance, prosecutors in certain cases, the Central Repository for certain sexual-offense information, law enforcement for registry-related records, and the Pardons Board if the person applies for a pardon. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-301/")
Wilma plain-language script:
Sealing helps with public access and many ordinary background checks, but it does not make the record invisible to every government agency or licensing body.

sourceSections.title: Filing process and forms
sourceSections.text:
Nevada procedures vary by county. Nevada State Police says a person should contact the court in the jurisdiction where the arrest occurred and obtain a Nevada criminal-history record to complete court forms. ("https://www.rccd.nv.gov/fees-forms/criminal-history-sealing-of-records/information-on-the-sealing-of-nevada-criminal-history-records/")
Typical packet components:
1 Petition to Seal Records
2 Affidavit or Declaration in Support of Petition
3 Stipulation to Seal Records for prosecutor review
4 Order to Seal Records
5 Current verified Nevada criminal-history report
6 Case dispositions / judgment / docket entries
7 Agency list for all record custodians
8 Civil cover sheet and fee waiver request, if required locally
Clark Countys District Attorney record-sealing instructions say the petition and order are prepared using criminal-history/SCOPE information, and the forms allow multiple arrest entries if each entry includes date of arrest, arresting agency, charge, and final disposition. ("https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/district_attorney/divisions/criminal/sealing_of_records/record_sealing_forms_instructions/")
Nevada Legal Services recommends obtaining the certified criminal-history record from the Nevada Department of Public Safety, checking local records first, filing in the appropriate court, sending the petition and stipulation to each prosecutor, and obtaining multiple certified copies of the signed order for all agencies that must seal their records. ("https://nevadalegalservices.org/criminal-record-sealing/")

9. Multiple records / multiple courts: NRS 179.2595
If a person wants to seal more than one record and would otherwise need to file in more than one court, they may file in district court for sealing of all such records. A district court may also order sealing of records in justice or municipal courts under the Nevada record-sealing statutes. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-2595/")
Wilma status: possible_single_district_court_petition_multiple_records
Wilma script:
If your records are spread across different Nevada courts, you may be able to use one district-court petition instead of separate petitions in every court.

sourceSections.title: Deferred judgment: NRS 176.211
sourceSections.text:
Nevada deferred judgment can allow a person to plead guilty, guilty but mentally ill, or no contest, but have judgment deferred. If the person completes the terms, the court discharges the person and dismisses the proceedings. The statute says the discharge/dismissal is without adjudication of guilt and is not a conviction for employment, civil-rights, licensing, questionnaire, and other public/private purposes, though it can still count for certain repeat-offense or bail purposes. The court must order records sealed if the person fulfills required terms, unless the Division or prosecutor petitions for good cause not to seal and requests a hearing. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-176/statute-176-211/")
Wilma status labels:possible_deferred_judgment_dismissal_176_211likely_sealable_after_deferred_judgment_completionneeds_deferred_judgment_order_and_completion_proof

sourceSections.title: Probation / specialty court dismissal and set-aside routes
sourceSections.text:
Under NRS 176A.245, 176A.265, and 176A.295, after certain probation discharges, dismissals, conditional dismissals, or set-asides, the court must order records sealed if the defendant fulfilled the terms and conditions, unless the Division petitions for good cause and requests a hearing. For certain domestic battery or DUI-related charges under these provisions, sealing may require waiting 7 years after conditional dismissal or set-aside and filing a petition. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-176a/statute-176a-245/")
Wilma status labels:possible_probation_discharge_sealingpossible_specialty_court_sealingdomestic_battery_or_dui_7_year_special_ruleneeds_probation_discharge_or_set_aside_order

sourceSections.title: Reentry program sealing: NRS 179.259
sourceSections.text:
Four years after an eligible person completes a reentry program, the court may seal records. The person must have successfully completed a qualifying reentry program and have been convicted of a single felony offense that did not involve use or threatened use of force or violence. Multiple felony convictions may count as a single offense if they arose out of the same transaction or occurrence. Crimes against children and sexual offenses are excluded. Professional licensing boards and the Division of Insurance may still inspect records sealed under this section for licensing/discipline purposes. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-259/")
Wilma status labels:possible_reentry_program_sealing_4_yearsexcluded_reentry_child_or_sex_offenselicensing_access_warning

13. Decriminalized offense: NRS 179.271
If an offense is later decriminalized, a person convicted before decriminalization may submit a written request to the court that convicted them. If the prosecutor does not object within 10 judicial days, the court must grant the request. If the prosecutor objects, the court holds a hearing and must grant unless the prosecutor proves good cause by clear and convincing evidence. No fee may be charged. This pathway does not apply to traffic offenses. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-271/")
Wilma status labels:possible_decriminalized_offense_no_feeexcluded_traffic_offense_decriminalization_routeprosecutor_10_judicial_day_objection_window

14. Controlled-substance possession not for sale: NRS 453.3365
For certain convictions for possession of a controlled substance not for sale, the court may seal records three years after conviction and sentencing if the person completed court/parole/probation terms and the court is satisfied after hearing that the person is rehabilitated. If the person was discharged from probation under NRS 453.3363, the court must order sealing if terms were fulfilled, unless Parole and Probation petitions for good cause not to seal and requests a hearing. Professional licensing boards may inspect records sealed under this section. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-453/statute-453-3365/")
Wilma status labels:possible_drug_possession_not_for_sale_453_3365possible_453_3363_discharge_sealinglicensing_board_access_warning

sourceSections.title: Trafficking-victim vacatur and sealing: NRS 179.247
sourceSections.text:
Nevada allows certain people to petition to vacate judgment and seal records if the conviction was for prostitution/solicitation-related conduct, certain nonviolent Nevada crimes, or loitering for prostitution/solicitation, and the persons participation resulted from being a victim of trafficking or involuntary servitude. If granted, the court vacates the judgment, dismisses the accusatory pleading, and seals related records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179/statute-179-247/")
Wilma status labels:possible_trafficking_victim_vacatur_and_sealinglegal_aid_handoff_trafficking_victimneeds_trafficking_or_involuntary_servitude_facts
Wilma script:
Nevada has a special relief pathway for some trafficking survivors. That should be routed to legal aid or an attorney because it can involve vacating the conviction, not just sealing it.

16. Favorable-disposition repository removal: NRS 179A.160
If a person was arrested, cited, or subject to a warrant and later acquitted or received a favorable disposition, they may apply in writing to the Central Repository and the agency maintaining the record to remove it from generally searched criminal-history files. The agency must remove it unless exceptions apply, including fugitive status, active prosecution, deferred prosecution/plea bargain-type disposition, a prior felony/gross misdemeanor conviction, or a new arrest/charge after the record at issue. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/nevada/chapter-179a/statute-179a-160/")
